coredata.dc:description: The predominance of micropores and micro-mesopores in conventional carbon materials often restricts molecular transport, limiting the accessibility of short-lived radicals to pollutants and reducing their effective utilization. To address this challenge, the pore structure of catalysts was regulated to facilitate the efficient mineralization of phenol. Firstly, a series of N-doped carbon nanotube membranes were grown on paper-like sintered stainless steel microfiber supports (PSSF) with different hierarchical porous structures. Secondly, the catalyst was characterized by FE-SEM, TEM, XRD, Raman, TG, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. Finally, the catalysts were applied for efficient phenol degradation via activation of peroxymonosulfonate (PMS) in a structured fixed-bed reactor. The results showed that catalysts with a higher mesopore content, increased porosity, and larger average pore diameter exhibited superior phenol degradation performance. The optimized pore structure provided abundant active sites and minimized both internal and external diffusion limitations. Moreover, the mineralization of byproducts improved catalyst stability. This research offers a new strategy for optimizing free radical utilization in water treatment.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube membrane with hierarchical porous structure for catalytic degradation of phenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The predominance of micropores and micro-mesopores in conventional carbon materials often restricts molecular transport, limiting the accessibility of short-lived radicals to pollutants and reducing their effective utilization. To address this challenge, the pore structure of catalysts was regulated to facilitate the efficient mineralization of phenol. Firstly, a series of N-doped carbon nanotube membranes were grown on paper-like sintered stainless steel microfiber supports (PSSF) with different hierarchical porous structures. Secondly, the catalyst was characterized by FE-SEM, TEM, XRD, Raman, TG, and nitrogen adsorption-desorption techniques. Finally, the catalysts were applied for efficient phenol degradation via activation of peroxymonosulfonate (PMS) in a structured fixed-bed reactor. The results showed that catalysts with a higher mesopore content, increased porosity, and larger average pore diameter exhibited superior phenol degradation performance. The optimized pore structure provided abundant active sites and minimized both internal and external diffusion limitations. Moreover, the mineralization of byproducts improved catalyst stability. This research offers a new strategy for optimizing free radical utilization in water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The evolution of metal-free carbon catalysts for advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) presents a critical pathway toward sustainable water remediation technologies [1–7]. While sp2-hybridized nanocarbons (e.g., CNTs, graphene) demonstrate exceptional peroxide activation capabilities through diverse pathways [5,8–13], their practical implementation faces fundamental engineering constraints. The prevailing strategy of employing powdered catalysts in batch systems—though achieving high pollutant degradation rates through intensive mixing—introduces operationally prohibitive challenges, including catalyst aggregation, irretrievable losses, and secondary ecotoxicity risks from nanomaterial dispersion [14–19]. These limitations starkly contrast with the process intensification requirements of continuous-flow industrial wastewater treatment.

Fixed-bed reactors emerge as an architecturally superior solution, offering enhanced solid-liquid contact efficiency and service life [20–22]. However, the direct translation of nanocarbon catalysts into such systems encounters intrinsic material limitations. Strong π-π interactions and van der Waals forces induce nanocarbon agglomeration, occluding active sites and compromising pore accessibility [23]. While the micropores and micro-mesoporous structures provide a wealth of active sites, the prevalent microporosity (<2 nm) in conventional carbon materials creates molecular transport bottlenecks [24–27]—particularly detrimental for AOP systems where short-lived radical species require optimized diffusion pathways to maximize pollutant oxidation efficiency [28–30]. This pore structure dichotomy creates a fundamental material design challenge: how to simultaneously preserve high-density active sites while enabling rapid mass transport of both reactants and byproducts.

Hierarchical porosity engineering provides a materials science solution to this process engineering dilemma. By integrating macropores (>50 nm) as molecular highways with mesopores (2–50 nm) for selective transport and micropores for active site exposure [31–34], this strategy can decouple the traditionally coupled variables of surface area and mass transfer efficiency [35–37]. Despite its demonstrated success in other fields (e.g., separation [38,39], sensing [40], energy storage [41]), the deliberate design of hierarchical porous carbons for AOPs—particularly in fixed-bed systems—remains unexplored.

Building on our previous study of PMS activation by NCNTm/PSSF [42,43]—which revealed mechanistic insights, but pore-related molecular transport limitations remain unexplored. We now focus on engineering the PSSF support’s pore hierarchy. By modulating the wet papermaking process, we constructed a hierarchical porous network to simultaneously optimize active site exposure and reactant transport. The resulting catalysts were tested in a fixed-bed reactor for continuous phenol degradation via PMS activation, with systematic analysis linking pore structure to performance. This work provides a promising strategy for the efficient utilization of free radicals and the removal of pollutants in a continuous system.
The information on all the chemicals is provided in Supporting Information (Text S1).

In order to design different hierarchical porous structures, simple adjustments and changes were made to the preparation process of PSSF. Based on previous work, PSSF was synthesized through papermaking and sintering processes [44]. During the nitrogen annealing phase, coniferous wood fibers underwent pyrolysis while stainless steel fibers were sintered, yielding a precursor with excellent mechanical strength and abundant macropores. To modify the macroporous structure, varying amounts of stainless steel fibers (3 g, 6 g, 9 g, 12 g, and 15 g) were used, ensuring sufficient mechanical strength of the final PSSF. The resulting PSSF precursors were designated as PSSF-3, PSSF-6, PSSF-9, PSSF-12, and PSSF-15, respectively. The experimental setup is depicted in Fig. 1. Prior to the reaction, the air in the quartz tube was purged with nitrogen. The central region of the quartz tube (Zone Ⅱ), where PSSF (1 g) was placed, was heated to 850 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min. Whereafter, the region containing the precursor (Zone Ⅰ) was heated to 200 °C to facilitate the volatilization of imidazole (3 g). The gaseous carbon and nitrogen active species generated by this volatilization were carried by nitrogen, diffused to the PSSF surface, and deposited to form NCNT. This chemical vapor deposition (CVD) process was maintained at 850 °C for 30 min, after which the furnace was cooled to room temperature under a nitrogen atmosphere. Throughout the procedure, nitrogen was supplied at a constant flow rate of 300 sccm. The synthetic hierarchical porous catalyst is referred to as NCNTm/PSSF-X according to the support. However, for collective analysis, the catalysts are referred to as NCNTm/PSSF. Notably, previous studies indicate that the optimal graphitization degree in metal-free catalysts is achieved with a 3:1 mass ratio of precursor to PSSF [45]. Therefore, to eliminate the effects of partial pressure variations, the mass of the precursor was strictly controlled at 3.000 g ± 0.001 g, and the weight of the PSSF at 1.000 g ± 0.010 g.
The characterization method of the catalysts is detailed in the Supporting Information (Text S2).

This study employed a structured fixed-bed reactor with NCNTm/PSSF as the catalytic bed for phenol degradation, simulating industrial wastewater treatment. A schematic diagram of the experimental setup is illustrated in Fig. 2. The reactor was packed axially with NCNTm/PSSF and peripherally with 2–3 mm glass beads to ensure fluid distribution uniformity and prevent channeling. The system operated under optimized conditions determined from prior kinetic studies: a 2 cm catalyst bed height, 2 mL/min feed flow rate, and a constant reaction temperature of 30°C maintained by a water bath. Phenol solution (100 mg/L) and peroxymonosulfonate (PMS, 3 g/L) were pumped upward through the reactor, with temperature maintained by a water bath [46]. Effluent samples were collected hourly, filtered (0.22 μm), and analyzed. Detailed calculations for phenol, PMS, and TOC conversion rates, along with EPR operational parameters, are provided in Supporting Information (Text S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of hierarchical porous membrane materials

ce:section.ce:para: The hierarchical pore structure of NCNTm/PSSF is observed by FE-SEM ( Fig. 3). Distinct macropores are evident within the interstitial spaces between stainless steel fibers, formed through their three-dimensional stacking configuration[47]. An inverse correlation between stainless steel fiber content and macropore dimensions was observed, with pore size reduction progressing sequentially from Sample a1 to e1 (Fig. 3(a1)-(e1)). This structural evolution stems from two synergistic mechanisms: (1) Enhanced fiber packing density during lamination and sintering processes at elevated metal content, which geometrically constrains pore formation; (2) Metal-mediated catalytic optimization wherein increased active sites promote efficient utilization of carbon/nitrogen precursors, facilitating denser NCNTm growth through vapor-solid deposition mechanisms.

Complementary TEM characterization (Fig. S1) resolves the nanotubular morphology, displaying hollow architectures with periodic corrugations and bamboo-like compartmentalization - morphological hallmarks of nitrogen incorporation into graphitic lattices [48]. HAADF-STEM (High-Angle Annular Dark-Field Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy) coupled with elemental mapping demonstrates the homogeneous spatial distribution of C, N, and O throughout the structure, as illustrated in Fig. 4. Notably, HAADF imaging identifies high-contrast nanoparticles confined within the nanotube cavities. HR-TEM analysis unambiguously identifies these encapsulated nanoparticles as Fe3C. The formation mechanism can be understood through the CVD growth process: carbon continuously dissolves into the alloy catalyst droplet until reaching saturation, at which point carbon nanotubes nucleate and grow. The observed Fe3C nanoparticles represent metastable phases that become trapped within the nanotubes due to the thermodynamic stability of Fe3C at elevated temperatures. According to Huang et al. [49], These Fe3C nanoparticles serve as additional catalytic sites, significantly enhancing the degradation efficiency of phenolic compounds.

In terms of mass transfer, macropores larger than 50 nm offer significant advantages by reducing diffusion barriers and shortening mass transfer pathways, thus enhancing efficiency and supporting high-throughput reaction processes [32]. On individual stainless steel fibers, as seen in Fig. 3 (a2) to (e2) and (a3) to (e3), NCNTm grew densely and uniformly on all five types of PSSF, indicating excellent coverage. There were also pore structures between these intertwined NCNT and had been identified in previous studies as mesopores with pore sizes ranging from 2 to 50 nm[50]. Zhao et al. highlighted that these mesopores could provide selective reaction pathways for guest molecules, which may be highly beneficial in treating complex water matrices[34].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were shown in Fig. 5(a), which revealed that all five types of NCNTm exhibited typical Type IV adsorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures [51]. It was also observed that at low relative pressures, adsorption capacity increased slowly, suggesting that mesopores were less abundant and that macropores dominated [50]. Moreover, the Brunauer Emmet Teller method (BET) was employed to calculate the specific surface area (SSA) of different NCNTm, as shown in Table 1. As the proportion of stainless steel fibers increased, the SSA of the catalysts exhibited a notable rise from 2.5311 cm²/g to 32.3913 cm²/g, attributed to the increased NCNT content. The SSA contributed by PSSF, being macroporous, was negligible; thus, the total SSA of the composite materials was predominantly provided by NCNT [49,52]. Therefore, as the density of stainless steel fiber increased, it could capture more carbon and nitrogen active species without changing the residence time during the process of chemical deposition and grow more NCNT, thus promoting the rise of SSA[46]. The evidence and analysis on the increase of NCNT load with the addition of stainless steel fiber consumption were also given in Fig. S2 and Table S1.

The pore size distribution of the mesoporous structure, determined by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, can be seen in Fig. 5(b). The pore size distribution curve showed that the pore size distribution of the five types of NCNTm was mainly distributed between 10 and 100 nm, and some samples had also detected micropores smaller than 2 nm, which might come from small pores in a broken multiwalled carbon nanotube graphite layer or small diameter NCNTs with tip openings[50]. However, as shown in Table 1, it was clear that the number of these micropores was small, and most of the pores were mesopores or macropores, which came from the gaps between NCNT[53]. In addition, the mesoporous volumes of each sample were further determined by the BJH method. Table 1 shows that the mesoporous volume increases greatly with the increase of support density. More NCNT formed intricate pores in the three-dimensional space. The outer surface of these mesopores provided sufficient sites for liquid phase reaction and played a role in connecting large pore channels to enhance reaction selectivity and material transfer[31,54]. However, the average pore diameter decreased from 26.9324 nm in NCNTm/PSSF-3–4.2698 nm in NCNTm/PSSF-15 due to the increase in support and NCNT density, indicating that the NCNTm on PSSF were more crowded. Li et al. believed that a small interstitial space could affect the diffusion of reactants, leading to a prolonged residence time in the bed. This would greatly increase the probability of secondary reactions and reduce the selectivity of the reaction, ultimately bringing pore blockage, carbon accumulation, and catalyst deactivation[55]. The differences between the above pores can be demonstrated by SEM images in Fig. S3.

Besides, the distribution of macropore size and porosity of the composite material was determined by the mercury intrusion method and is shown in Fig. 5(c). Similar to the characterization results of SEM (Fig. 3), the increase in the density of stainless steel in support resulted in a decrease in the pore size and quantity of macropores. Besides, due to the increase of metal sites, the higher loading NCNT further compressed the macropores in the three-dimensional space[56]. The dense NCNTm tended to replace the macropores in the original support through its mesoporous clusters. This resulted in a dramatic decrease in porosity, from 96.83 % (NCNTm/PSSF-3) to 55.24 % (NCNTm/PSSF-15)

Fig. 6 shows the results of the crystal structure of five types of NCNTm/PSSF by XRD. A low intensity peak was observed at 26.2°, which belonged to the (002) diffraction of vortex layer carbon[58]. The lower intensity indicated that the graphitization level of carbon in NCNTm was not high. This stemmed from the fact that a significant increase in hexagonal lattice disorder was caused by nitrogen doping. Moreover, triple strong peaks were detected at 2θ= 43.5°, 50.6°, 74.4°, which could be assigned to austenitic structures in 316 L stainless steel (PDF#33–0397). It was worth mentioning that all the samples had two diffraction peaks located at 37.6° and 63.3° according to the (112) and (301) planes of cementite (PDF#35–0772). The formation of cementite was caused by the dissolution of gaseous carbon and nitrogen active species into the liquid Fe catalyst, which then underwent a "gas-liquid-solid" process during the preparation of nanotubes using chemical vapor deposition[59].

The results of surface functional group properties of different NCNTm by FT-IR were shown in Fig. 7. Five types of NCNTm/PSSF revealed two strong broad peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1, corresponding to the valence oscillation of the CC bond in the benzene ring and the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups adsorbed on water or a few hydroxyl groups contained in NCNTm themselves, respectively [60–62]. At low wave number, NCNTm/PSSF presented some characteristic functional group peaks corresponding to N and O. To be more specific, the weaker peak at 1040 cm−1 was due to stretching vibrations of the C-N group [63]. The wide peak near 1120 cm−1 derived from the stretching vibration of C-O group [64,65]. The peak with a wave number of 1250 cm−1 corresponded to the bonded epoxy configuration in the graphite carbon configuration[66]. The weak peak at 1360 cm−1 could be attributed to the graphite-nitrogen (C-N = ) structure formed by effective doping of nitrogen in the hexagonal lattice [67–69]. The weak shoulder peak at 1510 cm−1 was assignable to a bit N-O groups formed by nitrogen oxygen binding, which was also consistent with the results of XPS below [70].

The results of microstructure and defects of NCNTm by using Raman were displayed in Fig. 8, both the D-band (∼1360 cm−1), related to disordered sp3 carbon configurations, and the G-band (∼1580 cm−1), associated with the graphitic sp2 carbon structure, were observed in all NCNTm/PSSF samples[71,72]. The strength ratio of D-band and G-band is an important indicator for evaluating the degree of defects in carbon materials[73]. The ID/IG of the five kinds of NCNTm remained within the range of 0.90–1.10 without significant discrepancy, indicating that the differences in pore structure in PSSF did not noticeably affect the bonding of nitrogen elements in the hexagonal lattice during chemical vapor deposition. On the other hand, our previous studies had also confirmed that the appropriate support/precursor ratio (3:1) guaranteed that there were sufficient metal active sites at the "encapsulation concentration" to dissolve gaseous carbon and nitrogen species and precipitated NCNT during the reaction process, thus ensuring that the introduction of defects in the five NCNTm was similar[45]. It was worth mentioning that the 2D peak corresponding to a long-range ordered structure with a wave number of 2700 cm−1 was not observed in the species NCNTm, which also confirmed that nitrogen doping seriously disrupted the ordered graphene layer in CNT[50].

The high-resolution C1s spectra of the five samples and the proportions of the various carbon configurations by XPS were displayed in Fig. 9, Fig. 10 (a) and (b), respectively. It could be observed that the proportion of surface carbon elements in all samples reached over 85 %. The dominating peaks of 284.6 ± 0.2 eV and 285.2 ± 0.5 eV corresponded to the sp2 C (C1) and aliphatic sp3 C (C2) configurations, respectively, where the presence of sp3 C configuration represented lattice defects and disturbances in ordered carbon materials[74]. They may stem from the amorphous carbon in NCNTm/PSSF itself or the disorder caused by nitrogen doping[75]. Due to the presence of oxygen containing gases in the environment and oxygen elements in the PSSF support, some carbon oxygen functional groups such as C-O, CO and COOH (C3) were also obtained in the higher binding energy region. However, considering the doping of nitrogen elements, the peaks at the above positions might also come from C-N or CN[76]. Moreover, the weak peak attributed to π - π transition (C4) was also observed in some samples in the region of binding energy of 290.0 ± 0.5 eV[77,78].

Further information about oxygen content and bonding types on the NCNTm/PSSF surface could be obtained by deconvolution of O1s spectra were shown in Fig. 9, Fig. 10 (a) and (c), respectively. The surface oxygen content of all the samples did not exceed 5 %. The peaks of binding energy of 530.0 ± 0.3 eV and 535.5 ± 0.3 eV are attributed to underutilized metal-oxygen bonds (O1) and water adsorbed on the sample surface (O5)[52,79,80]. In the middle of the two peaks, the peaks of typical oxygen-containing functional groups attributed to CO (O2), C-O (O3) and COOH (O4) were detected at 530.9 ± 0.2 eV, 531.9 ± 0.2 eV and 533.5 ± 0.3 eV, respectively[81,82]. In the above configuration, the proportion of O2, O3 and O4 was high while the proportion of O1 and O5 was relatively low.

Figs. 9, 10 (a) and (d) provided high-resolution N1s spectra, surface nitrogen content and bonding types of different NCNTm/PSSF. It could be observed that the surface nitrogen content of the five composite materials was above 7 %, and there was no significant fluctuation. This stability could be attributed to the same preparation process of the five composite materials. The N1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 398.6 eV, 400.9 eV, 403.2 eV, and 405.2 eV, which were consistent with pyridine nitrogen (N1), graphite nitrogen (N2), encapsulated molecular nitrogen (N3), and nitrogen oxides chemically adsorbed on the graphite layer (N4), indicating that a portion of nitrogen was alright doped into the carbon molecular skeleton (graphite nitrogen) and edge or vacancy (pyridine nitrogen). This part of the nitrogen configuration could promote the formation of free radicals by promoting the adsorption of PMS as well as subsequent electron transfer, and it was very beneficial to the removal of pollutants.[48,83–85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenol degradation in a fixed bed reactor
Performance of activating PMS for phenol degradation by different hierarchical porous membrane catalysts as bed
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.S4 presented the adsorption capacity of five types of NCNTm/PSSF for phenol without the addition of PMS. The driving force of adsorption of phenol by NCNTm/PSSF was mainly from electron coupling, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions[86]. It could be seen that all catalysts exhibited poor adsorption performance for phenol due to their high porosity and excessive macroporous structure. The NCNTm/PSSF-15 presented the highest adsorption capacity, which could continuously adsorb about 30 % of phenol within a 7-hour reaction. However, the adsorption and removal level of phenol in other samples was less than 20 %. As shown in Fig. 11, PSSF with excellent acid corrosion resistance exhibits no intrinsic catalytic activity toward PMS activation and therefore cannot independently function as an AOPs catalyst. The observed catalytic performance of NCNTm/PSSF composites originates exclusively from the incorporated NCNTm components.

As displayed in Fig. 11 (a), the removal capacity of NCNTm/PSSF for phenol significantly increased after adding PMS to the raw material solution. Among them, NCNTm/PSSF-9 had the best ability to activate PMS to remove phenol, which could almost completely remove phenol within 7 hours and revealed excellent stability. In contrast, the catalytic performance of other catalysts revealed remarkable differences. When NCNTm/PSSF-3 was implemented as a catalyst, the phenol conversion at first hour was only 55.2 %. However, as the reaction progressed, the phenol conversion gradually increased and reached 90.3 % after 7 hours of reaction. This might be due to the acidic environment during the reaction process promoting the opening of the NCNT tip, thereby facilitating the improvement of the contact efficiency between phenol and reactive oxygen species. This phenomenon of gradually improving catalytic performance was also observed when NCNTm/PSSF-6 was employed as beds. However, when NCNTm/PSSF-12 was used as beds, the time node with the highest phenol conversion was the first few hours of the reaction. As the reaction progressed, the conversion of phenol sharply decreased and could only be maintained at 49.2 % after 7 hours of reaction. This phenomenon was more pronounced when the bed was NCNTm/PSSF-15.

TOC conversion in Fig. 11(b) revealed that when NCNTm/PSSF-6 and NCNTm/PSSF-9 were used as beds, The TOC conversion were maintained at 82 % and 70 % respectively, and remained stable throughout the entire reaction process. In contrast, the TOC conversion of NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15 as catalysts was very low, only about 30 % and 20 %. In order to further highlight the difference of catalytic performance of phenol in different beds, the concentration curve of intermediate product was obtained as shown in Fig. 11 (c) by calculating the discrepancy between TOC conversion and phenol conversion at each time node. It could be observed that when NCNTm/PSSF-3, NCNTm/PSSF-6 and NCNTm/PSSF-9 were adopted as catalyst beds, the concentration of intermediate products was almost negligible. However, the concentration of intermediate products could reach tens of milligrams when NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15 were employed as catalyst bed. which revealed that the oxidation of phenol by active oxygen species in the bed was extremely inadequate. The difference in the concentration of intermediates might be attributed to the difference in the proportion of macropores. A higher proportion of macropores and a proper number of mesopores could effectively transport relative oxygen species on the basis of ensuring sufficient active site at a higher liquid time space velocity[87]. This would promote the contact of phenol and its intermediates with reactive oxygen species more completely and accelerate the efficiency of the reaction byproducts from the beds. In contrast, the pores of NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15 were narrower, and the removal of reactant transport and external diffusion was not sufficient. This might lead to the rapid formation of carbon deposits and hinder the diffusion of subsequent reactants, thus reducing the selectivity of the desired product[41,88]. Besides, it was easy to bring the accumulation of some intermediates in the continuous flow treatment system, which may cause incomplete oxidation of phenol[89].

In addition, the conversion of PMS during the reaction was further monitored by iodimetry. Surprisingly, Fig. 11 (d) revealed that the conversion of PMS in the experimental group dominated by NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15 were highly variable. As the reaction progressed, the conversion of PMS dropped by tens of percentage points, reaching only 60 % in the later stage of the reaction, indicating that the active sites of the above two catalysts were seriously insufficient or difficult to reach in the later stage of the reaction. In order to prove this point, NCNTm/PSSF-15 was taken as an example to conduct SEM analysis, and the results were shown in Fig. 12. It could be observed that with the progress of the reaction, a large number of sediments gradually accumulated on the surface of the catalyst, which seriously damaged the excellent three-dimensional network structure of the catalyst. Subsequent EDS mapping analysis in Fig.S5 confirmed that these sediments were mainly composed of carbon oxides and combined with a certain amount of sulfur species. They most likely came from intermediates with incomplete mineralization and byproducts of PMS. At the macro level, these deposits made it difficult for subsequent PMS in the feedstock solution to access the active nitrogen configuration in the NCNTm and hinder the mediated electron transfer process. Besides, excess oxygen also greatly reduced the conjugated aromaticity of the graphite network and reduces the reaction efficiency through steric hindrance effect.

In order to detect reactive oxygen species in the reaction process, EPR technology was implemented to monitor the free radicals in the reaction process, and the results were shown in Fig. 13. It could be observed that the EPR spectra of the NCNTm/PSSF-3, NCNTm/PSSF-6 and NCNTm/PSSF-9 groups exhibited a typical quadruple peak with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1, corresponding to the peaks of DMPO-·OH, the addition product of DMPO and ·OH. At the same time, signals of SO4 •- and the addition product DMPO- SO4 •- were also observed, but their intensity was very weak. This was because DMPO had a much stronger ability to capture • OH than SO4 •- [90]. The appearance of the above peak patterns demonstrated the presence of abundant free radical species in the beds that could be contacted[29]. Their abundant presence was beneficial for the deep oxidation of phenol. In contrast, the EPR spectra of the experimental group dominated by NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15 showed a six-fold peak with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1:1:1. The total spectral width of the six-fold peaks was 56 G, which was characteristic of the addition peaks of DMPO and carbon-based free radicals—DMPO-※C[91–93]. However, no signal of addition peak of other free radicals with DMPO was detected. suggesting that DMPO was bound to a large number of intermediates in the system, and also indicates that the content of ·OH and SO4 •- in these experimental groups was low.

Although some studies have pointed out that the specific surface area and mesopore volume have a crucial impact on catalytic activity [94], it could be seen from Fig. 11 that each experimental group could fully activate PMS within a few hours before the reaction although their above two indicators are not high, indicating that the active sites in NCNTm/PSSF were completely sufficient. The rapid consumption of PMS resulted in a large concentration gradient between the main body of the reaction solution and the active site of the catalyst[95].According to Fick's law, this would promote the diffusion of phenol and PMS more rapidly from the main body of the liquid membrane to the boundary and even inside the catalyst. Jiang et al. revealed that the diffusion distance of the reactants in the catalyst with macropore structure was much shorter than that in the bed of the same size without macropore structure[54,55]. The abundant large hole structure was like a "highway", ensuring the rapid accessibility of the active surface, so that external diffusion could be well eliminated even at low ambient temperatures and airspeeds [96], facilitating free radical enrichment and molecular diffusion between the liquid membrane surface and the catalyst fixed interface[28].

However, with the progress of the reaction, the utilization of PMS in different experimental groups showed a discrepancy, which directly caused the difference in the conversion of phenol. Compared with NCNTm/PSSF-12 and NCNTm/PSSF-15, NCNTm/PSSF-3, NCNTm/PSSF-6 and NCNTm/PSSF-9 with more macropores could activate and consume PMS for a longer period of time, thus generating free radicals for deep oxidation of phenol. In addition, the smaller interstitial space (average pore diameter) and lower porosity in NCNT-12 and NCNT-15 would lead to excessive density of active sites, which was not conducive to the elimination of internal diffusion. Besides, the resulting low effective diffusion coefficient would also cause the byproducts of activated PMS and the intermediate products after oxidation of phenol to block and gradually accumulate on the three-dimensional pore surface constructed by NCNTm, which isolated the active site inside the support and ultimately reduce the catalytic activity.

The influence of external diffusion on the reaction seemed to determine the upper limit of the reaction, while the influence of internal diffusion on the reaction was mainly whether the reaction was durable and whether the catalyst had long-term stability. After reacting as catalyst beds for several hours, the surface of NCNTm/PSSF was coated with numerous carbon and oxygen species and gradually lost the ability to activate PMS. It could be considered that with the progress of diffusion, the concentration of reactants at each point inside the pore gradually decreased due to the overcoming of diffusion resistance and reaction consumption, and the reaction rate also dropped accordingly. Therefore, when the reactant concentration was the lowest or equal to zero at the center of pores, the reaction rate was also the smallest. NCNTm/PSSF-3, NCNTm/PSSF-6 and NCNTm/PSSF-9 had high porosity and interstitial space, so they only needed to ensure a small concentration difference between the inside and outside of the pore to supply the reactants consumed by the reaction on the inner surface of the pore. At this time, the concentration of reactants in contact with the pore wall was almost equal to the concentration at the pore, which did not cause more reaction materials to accumulate on the surface of the pore like a membrane as shown in Fig. 14, resulting in catalyst poisoning[97,98]. In addition, the blockage of the catalyst pore would greatly increasd the mass transfer resistance and seriously inhibited the reactivity, thus leading to a variety of adverse fluid phenomena such as channel flow, wall flow and backmixing, and shorten the life of the catalyst[99].

In conclusion, five types of hierarchical porous microfiber composite nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube membrane (NCNTm/PSSF) were prepared by chemical vapor deposition on PSSF supports with different stainless steel fiber contents. These membrane catalysts exhibited distinct macropore and mesopore volumes, average pore diameters, and porosities, which influenced their catalytic activities in phenol oxidation in a fixed-bed reactor. The mesopores within the NCNTm provided abundant active sites for the reaction, while the stainless steel fibers facilitated rapid reactant transfer. The catalytic performance varied significantly based on the membrane’s structural features. Membranes catalyst with insufficient macropore content, such as NCNTm/PSSF-15 and NCNTm/PSSF-12, demonstrated poor phenol and TOC removal and produced numerous intermediates. This was attributed to hindered diffusion pathways and the blockage of pores by reaction byproducts due to small average pore diameters. In contrast, NCNTm/PSSF-3, NCNTm/PSSF-6, and NCNTm/PSSF-9, with larger macropore volumes, enhanced the mass transfer of reactants, thus improving the catalytic performance and enabling more efficient phenol degradation. The optimized mesopore-macropore hierarchical structure facilitated deep oxidation by improving the distribution and contact efficiency of free radicals. The rational pore structure reduced the migration path of free radicals to a certain extent and facilitated the increase of their abundance in the bed and the improvement of their contact efficiency with phenol, thus promoting the deep oxidation of phenol. The hierarchical porous structure proposed in this paper provides a new idea for rapid advanced oxidation of pollutants.
